title: Combination Sum
code: public IList<IList<int>> CombinationSum(int[] candidates, int target)
for (int i = start; i < candidates.Length; i++)

frames.caption: Find all combinations of candidates summing to 7, each candidate reusable. Sort first, then DFS the choose/explore/un-choose tree, pruning any branch whose remaining goes below 0.
frames.note: The state-space tree branches on which candidate to add next; depth is bounded by target/min(candidate). Sorting the candidates lets a single overshoot break the whole loop, since everything to the right is larger. We record only at the leaves where remaining == 0, not at every node (unlike subsets).

frames.caption: CHOOSE candidates[0]=2 -> path [2], remaining 5. Explore Backtrack(start=0).
frames.note: CHOOSE candidates[i] and subtract it from remaining, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i (NOT i+1). Passing i again is what allows the same candidate to be reused unlimited times; never going backward (start never decreases) is what stops [2,3] and [3,2] both appearing.

frames.caption: CHOOSE candidates[0]=2 -> path [2,2], remaining 3. Explore Backtrack(start=0).
frames.note: CHOOSE candidates[i] and subtract it from remaining, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i (NOT i+1). Passing i again is what allows the same candidate to be reused unlimited times; never going backward (start never decreases) is what stops [2,3] and [3,2] both appearing.

frames.caption: CHOOSE candidates[0]=2 -> path [2,2,2], remaining 1. Explore Backtrack(start=0).
frames.note: CHOOSE candidates[i] and subtract it from remaining, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i (NOT i+1). Passing i again is what allows the same candidate to be reused unlimited times; never going backward (start never decreases) is what stops [2,3] and [3,2] both appearing.

frames.caption: candidates[0]=2 > remaining=1 -> BREAK (sorted, so the rest overshoot too).
frames.note: PRUNE on overshoot: because candidates are SORTED, the first candidate that exceeds remaining means every later (larger) one does too — so we break, killing the whole rest of this level at once. This is the single most important optimization here; an unsorted version could only continue, not break.

frames.caption: Back at start=0, i=0: UN-CHOOSE 2 -> path [2,2], remaining 3 again. Try the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the candidate back off the path and restore remaining so the next sibling branch starts clean. This rewind is what makes it backtracking. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: CHOOSE candidates[1]=3 -> path [2,2,3], remaining 0. Explore Backtrack(start=1).
frames.note: CHOOSE candidates[i] and subtract it from remaining, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i (NOT i+1). Passing i again is what allows the same candidate to be reused unlimited times; never going backward (start never decreases) is what stops [2,3] and [3,2] both appearing.

frames.caption: remaining == 0 -> RECORD [2,2,3] (result #1).
frames.note: BASE CASE: remaining hit exactly 0, so the path sums to target — record a COPY of it. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion unwinds, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: Back at start=0, i=1: UN-CHOOSE 3 -> path [2,2], remaining 3 again. Try the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the candidate back off the path and restore remaining so the next sibling branch starts clean. This rewind is what makes it backtracking. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: candidates[2]=6 > remaining=3 -> BREAK (sorted, so the rest overshoot too).
frames.note: PRUNE on overshoot: because candidates are SORTED, the first candidate that exceeds remaining means every later (larger) one does too — so we break, killing the whole rest of this level at once. This is the single most important optimization here; an unsorted version could only continue, not break.

frames.caption: Back at start=0, i=0: UN-CHOOSE 2 -> path [2], remaining 5 again. Try the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the candidate back off the path and restore remaining so the next sibling branch starts clean. This rewind is what makes it backtracking. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: CHOOSE candidates[1]=3 -> path [2,3], remaining 2. Explore Backtrack(start=1).
frames.note: CHOOSE candidates[i] and subtract it from remaining, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i (NOT i+1). Passing i again is what allows the same candidate to be reused unlimited times; never going backward (start never decreases) is what stops [2,3] and [3,2] both appearing.

frames.caption: candidates[1]=3 > remaining=2 -> BREAK (sorted, so the rest overshoot too).
frames.note: PRUNE on overshoot: because candidates are SORTED, the first candidate that exceeds remaining means every later (larger) one does too — so we break, killing the whole rest of this level at once. This is the single most important optimization here; an unsorted version could only continue, not break.

frames.caption: Back at start=0, i=1: UN-CHOOSE 3 -> path [2], remaining 5 again. Try the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the candidate back off the path and restore remaining so the next sibling branch starts clean. This rewind is what makes it backtracking. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: candidates[2]=6 > remaining=5 -> BREAK (sorted, so the rest overshoot too).
frames.note: PRUNE on overshoot: because candidates are SORTED, the first candidate that exceeds remaining means every later (larger) one does too — so we break, killing the whole rest of this level at once. This is the single most important optimization here; an unsorted version could only continue, not break.

frames.caption: Back at start=0, i=0: UN-CHOOSE 2 -> path [], remaining 7 again. Try the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the candidate back off the path and restore remaining so the next sibling branch starts clean. This rewind is what makes it backtracking. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: CHOOSE candidates[1]=3 -> path [3], remaining 4. Explore Backtrack(start=1).
frames.note: CHOOSE candidates[i] and subtract it from remaining, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i (NOT i+1). Passing i again is what allows the same candidate to be reused unlimited times; never going backward (start never decreases) is what stops [2,3] and [3,2] both appearing.

frames.caption: CHOOSE candidates[1]=3 -> path [3,3], remaining 1. Explore Backtrack(start=1).
frames.note: CHOOSE candidates[i] and subtract it from remaining, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i (NOT i+1). Passing i again is what allows the same candidate to be reused unlimited times; never going backward (start never decreases) is what stops [2,3] and [3,2] both appearing.

frames.caption: candidates[1]=3 > remaining=1 -> BREAK (sorted, so the rest overshoot too).
frames.note: PRUNE on overshoot: because candidates are SORTED, the first candidate that exceeds remaining means every later (larger) one does too — so we break, killing the whole rest of this level at once. This is the single most important optimization here; an unsorted version could only continue, not break.

frames.caption: Back at start=1, i=1: UN-CHOOSE 3 -> path [3], remaining 4 again. Try the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the candidate back off the path and restore remaining so the next sibling branch starts clean. This rewind is what makes it backtracking. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: candidates[2]=6 > remaining=4 -> BREAK (sorted, so the rest overshoot too).
frames.note: PRUNE on overshoot: because candidates are SORTED, the first candidate that exceeds remaining means every later (larger) one does too — so we break, killing the whole rest of this level at once. This is the single most important optimization here; an unsorted version could only continue, not break.

frames.caption: Back at start=0, i=1: UN-CHOOSE 3 -> path [], remaining 7 again. Try the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the candidate back off the path and restore remaining so the next sibling branch starts clean. This rewind is what makes it backtracking. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: CHOOSE candidates[2]=6 -> path [6], remaining 1. Explore Backtrack(start=2).
frames.note: CHOOSE candidates[i] and subtract it from remaining, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i (NOT i+1). Passing i again is what allows the same candidate to be reused unlimited times; never going backward (start never decreases) is what stops [2,3] and [3,2] both appearing.

frames.caption: candidates[2]=6 > remaining=1 -> BREAK (sorted, so the rest overshoot too).
frames.note: PRUNE on overshoot: because candidates are SORTED, the first candidate that exceeds remaining means every later (larger) one does too — so we break, killing the whole rest of this level at once. This is the single most important optimization here; an unsorted version could only continue, not break.

frames.caption: CHOOSE candidates[3]=7 -> path [7], remaining 0. Explore Backtrack(start=3).
frames.note: CHOOSE candidates[i] and subtract it from remaining, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i (NOT i+1). Passing i again is what allows the same candidate to be reused unlimited times; never going backward (start never decreases) is what stops [2,3] and [3,2] both appearing.

frames.caption: remaining == 0 -> RECORD [7] (result #2).
frames.note: BASE CASE: remaining hit exactly 0, so the path sums to target — record a COPY of it. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion unwinds, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: Back at start=0, i=3: UN-CHOOSE 7 -> path [], remaining 7 again. Try the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the candidate back off the path and restore remaining so the next sibling branch starts clean. This rewind is what makes it backtracking. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: Done: 2 combinations sum to 7 -> [2,2,3], [7].
frames.note: Only two combinations work: [2,2,3] (reusing 2 twice) and [7]. The start-index forward-only rule guarantees each combination appears once; recursing with i (not i+1) is what permitted reusing 2. Time is exponential and output-bound; space is O(target/min) for the recursion stack.
